posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
• 2015 Joh. Jos. Prüm Graacher Himmelreich Riesling Kabinett - Germany, Mosel Saar Ruwer (3/13/2025)
    Opened to raise a glass to Rudi Wiest, whose Cellars International imported the wine. From 375ml it was open from first pour, which was a big relief after my prior bottle several years ago was shut down hard. Lots of fresh fruit, and there’s a hint of creaminess to the texture, so it’s maturing if slowly. Surprising brightness, as 2015 always had some meat on its bones, so again age is conveying benefits. I will check in again soon, as this seems like it’s entering a good drinking window.

link_counts.title: Community Tasting Note - 2015 Joh. Jos. Prüm Graacher Himmelreich Riesling Kabinett - CellarTracker

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked:
$25/btl upon release. I bought just under a case and it wasn’t nearly enough! I saw a 2023 Prum WS Kabinett on the shelves today for $75.

$25 may indeed be underpriced but $75 is lunacy.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Between price escalation and potential tariffs I am glad to have as much Prum as I do. I think I am set for life (and afterlife!).

posts.name: Colby Scott
posts.cooked: Was fretting about how much Riesling I purchased last year (way beyond any semblance of a budget), but no longer. Seems like a blessing in disguise now.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: No way I could have too much Riesling.

posts.name: Tanner Schmucker
posts.cooked:
I envy all of you who were purchasing wine when Prum was <$30. Before Riesling was cool again. With everything going on we’ll be lucky to get Prum WS Kabi for $70 in a few years from now…

On a happier note, I just opened a 2020 WS Kabi the other day. I was worried it might be shut down, but judging from the CT notes it was fine to open. That it was. Expressive, complex, and a joy to drink. It had this wonderful chrysanthemum flower or jasmine thing going on, newly ripe green apple and river stones. It was my first Prum and I’m glad I experienced it in its relative youth first.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
David_Bu3ker:

Between price escalation and potential tariffs I am glad to have as much Prum as I do. I think I am set for life (and afterlife!)

Saw that and thought, hmmm, wonder how many David thinks is enough Prum for life so I compared how many I have to how many I have and was surprised that I own more Prum than you do! (Not more Selbach though.)

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I haven’t added the bottles from my dad’s cellar to my inventory yet!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Good to hear, have a bunch of this!

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: Haven’t touched any 2015 Prüm yet. I might do a 10-yr check in. I only have Sonnenuhr - Kab, Spat, Aus, Aus GK.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: The core four!

posts.name: Eric Dinnocenzo
posts.cooked: Last night I had the 2015 Prum Graacher Auslese at a wine dinner with 6 people and it was fantastic - the favorite wine of the night.

posts.name: Colby Scott
posts.cooked: We had that bottle during our Rieslingiving last November and felt the same. Definitely in its prime drinking window.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
Jayson_Cohen:

I only have Sonnenuhr - Kab, Spat, Aus, Aus GK.

Mistake. IMHO, the GH (at least at the kabinett/spatlese level) site produces Prum’s best wines.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: To each their own, Maureen. I prefer the WS. And if I am buying and drinking GH, which I do on occasion, I’m leaning Schaefer.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: Ha! I compared to and lets just say I think I will be ok. Plus JJ Prum is one of the easiest to get well stored back vintages.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Agreed. Prum is the #1 producer in my cellar in terms of bottles owned, but most of them are 2012 and before.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Colby_Scott:

Was fretting about how much Riesling I purchased last year (way beyond any semblance of a budget), but no longer. Seems like a blessing in disguise now.

I have too much Riesling. Five of the top ten producers in my cellar are German wines (Prum, Reinhold Haart, Zilliken, von Schubert and Falkenstein). Overall, I have more Burgundy in my cellar than anything else (four of the top 10 - Truchot, Rossignol-Trapet, Jadot and Ramonet) but still have a lot of German wines.

[Forget Prum at $25 or $75. When I first started buying Prum (long gone 1983 WS Spatlese), I paid $7-8. Just proves I am old.]

Need to remedy that by drinking it.

posts.name: Charles Weiss
posts.cooked: Sounds like we’re the same vintage, Howard. Though I’d been unable to stop buying Prum until after 2019, mostly in an attempt to make up for not appreciating Kabinett enough in the earlier days. Wish I still had some of the 1983.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked: Not sure I am appreciating the meaning of your statement (not the last sentence). In any event, my holdings from WS outnumber my holdings from GH by more than 2:1. But I think that is due in part to the fact that I reach for GH more readily than WS. Very generally speaking, I find GH wines to be a bit brighter, whereas the WS wines are a bit creamier.
